narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of first-time success on the bar exam based on students' academic records, test scores, and backgrounds, estimated a 58.9% chance that you would not pass it on the first try. This prediction draws from patterns seen in thousands of past students, where those with similar profiles faced higher hurdles in bar performance. For you, as a Black male with a middle-income family background, the model weighed your undergraduate GPA of 2.90—below the typical 3.21 average—and your Year 1 and Year 3 school deciles both at 4, which are lower than the averages of about 5.5 to 5.7 for admitted students. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your LSAT score of 22.50, well below the dataset average of 36.70, as lower scores like yours have historically correlated strongly with bar exam struggles in the model's analysis. On a more positive note, your undergraduate GPA actually worked in your favor, pulling the prediction somewhat toward passing success compared to weaker GPAs in similar cases. Your middle family income bracket also nudged things slightly toward a better outcome, reflecting that students from such backgrounds sometimes outperform expectations. Overall, these top three elements—dominated by the LSAT's strong negative pull—tipped the scales against admission, even though you're planning full-time studies like over 90% of students in the dataset. This isn't a judgment on your potential or resilience, especially given the unique challenges many Black students navigate in competitive academic pipelines. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to or above the average, as that single change could dramatically shift future predictions. Strengthening your academic deciles through additional coursework or a post-bac program might also help demonstrate upward momentum. We encourage reapplying after addressing these key areas, and resources like LSAT prep courses or academic advising are available to support you. Your determination can rewrite this story.
